title: Erich E.A. Mueller
caption: Erich, a self-described Christian socialist, lost his career in pecan polishing to automation late in the 19th century. His political writings figured into a defamation lawsuit by a Lutheran minister against his congregation.
Erich E.A. Mueller lived at 1408 Ferry Street in St. Louis. The building was still standing and inhabited in 2014, though badly dilapidated.

Bethlehem Lutheran Church, at North Florissant and Salisbury Street, excommunicated Erich E.A. Mueller during a period of congregational strife. Damaged by lightning in 1989, necessary repairs had reached $3 million in 1995, when the congregation relocated to the basement of its school building. The roof and a wall collapsed Friday, April 11, 2014, and the sanctuary was later demolished. Worship continues in the school. This photo was taken in 1998.

Bethlehem Lutheran Church as Erich E.A. Mueller would have known it ca. 1904. Dedicated October 29, 1893, the church was destroyed by fire three months later. It was immediately rebuilt, following the original plan and style. When the twin steeples were destroyed in the tornado of 1927, they were not rebuilt but simply squared off.

title: *Familie Müller* arrives at Castle Garden
caption: The 11-member Mueller family arrived in New York harbor on Sunday, October 1, according to Fritz’s journal, and went ashore at Castle Garden the next day. They traveled in first cabin and were the first people listed on Capt. B. Forstmann’s 362-passenger manifest given under oath to Port of New York authorities. All the Muellers, even the small children, were listed as German farmers headed for Indiana: Fritz Müller, male, 42 (note umlaut in U.S. records); Johanna, female, 45; Theodora, female, 21; Lisabeth, female, 20; Wilhelm, male, 18; Erich, male, 16; Emma, female, 15; Friederich, male, 10; Enno, male [mistakenly entered as female], 9; Hans, male, 6; and Gertrude, female, 5. The listed ages likely reflect the point at which Fritz booked passage, since, Fritz, Wilhelm, Erich, Friederich, Hans, and Gertrude were actually a year older on arrival than their recorded ages.

title: In Amerika ist alles herrlich

**In Brooklyn, June 20, 2015**   With great color, an international gathering of family and friends, about 50 fellow Brown graduates, and a ceremony that seamlessly merged traditions, Anson and Reva were married at the Green Building in Brooklyn.

Larry Bigelow was a prolific watercolorist and an undercover agent for the wartime OSS and later for the CIA. He spoke several languages and traveled widely for the CIA — India, Afghanistan, Vietnam, Africa. He painted everywhere.
**View from Manitou**   Molly Bigelow McMillan wrote about childhood summers on Manitou Island in the ’20s and ’30s. The image, from the Bigelow residence ca. 1920, is familiar to generations.

**Black Pine, Montana, 1913**   Black Pine was home to at least five generations of Rehnlunds, a hardy family who emigrated from Pite√•, Sweden, above the Arctic Circle. Tim and Nancy Nickel visited in 1977 and received this image from photographer Frank Niels.
**The Friendly Farm**   Ernst and Erna Marquardt’s dairy barn — “Box 43” — now derelict. The Freedom church is half a mile or so down the road at left. Photographed June 7, 2007, by Mark Nickel.

**Dairy barn**   Among the many unforgettable wonders of rural southern Minnesota was the sensory delight of milking time at Box 43: the hum of the motor, the rhythmic surging of compressed air from the milkers, the smell of manure, hay, and silage, and the relentless chewing of large patient animals.

**First home**   Carl de Haas, Richard McMillan’s great-grandfather, emigrated to Calumet, Wisconsin, in 1847. This image of his first homestead comes from *Winke fur Auswanderer*, his book of advice for emigrants, widely sold in Germany.
Molly turned 90 in ’09 and three generations were in town to celebrate. (Still to come: Leo Henry (2012), Rhys Lewis (2014), Kyle Richard (2017), Mabel (2017), and Hartfield Oscar (2020).

Bill and Esther Crawley acquired some land near Usquepaug, Rhode Island, in the 1940s. Rustic and largely undeveloped, the land and the “country house” passed to Dan and Susan Crawley on Esther’s death. It is now part of the Richmond and South Kingstown Land Trusts.

**Love the hats**   Logan, Jenn, Maggie, and Dan sent 2021 New Year’s greetings with notes of near constant activity: backpacking, biking, fishing, long runs, rowing, paddle boarding, skiing, hiking and more.

**Situate in the upper fields**   In 1853 William Dorris stopped by to see his birthplace, the house his father had built in 1809. He made this sketch and mailed it to his daughter Annie. It was near the Mullock River in Ballykeel Mourne, County Down, Ireland, “situate in the upper fields that my father then owned.”

***Unser Heim und Heimat***    Friedrich “Fritz” Mueller sold this Bosenbüttel estate — fields, sawmill, brick factory — moved his fortune to America through Bremen banks, and emigrated with his wife and nine children in August 1865 on the wooden sailing ship *Der Adler*.
